steps.text: Collect VFCs in evacuated passivated silicon ceramic lined stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent.
Ensure field sampling and recovery staff are trained in best practices for extracting representative gas samples from an emission vent or stack.
Condition the gas if necessary to manage moisture and acid gases.
Collect the gas sample in passivated stainless-steel canisters.
Heat PFA sample lines to the maximum operating temperature (≥150 °C/300 °F) and flush with nitrogen or zero air for approximately two hours.
Sample lines used for previous sampling can be heated and flushed as in Section 8.4.3.1 before re-use.
A line that may be highly contaminated can be rinsed with methanol then heated and flushed as in Section 8.4.3.1.
Alternatively, new PFA sample tubing may be used inside a larger diameter heated tube such that the new tubing can be replaced after each use.
Determine which sampling system configuration is appropriate for sampling (Section 8.1, 8.2, or 8.3).
Determine stack gas moisture content.
For non-combustion sources, determine if acid gases are suspected or are present based on process knowledge.
For gases from sources where the moisture is >3% v/v and/or acid gases are present, the VFC Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management is used.
For gases from sources where the moisture is confirmed to be ≤3% v/v and acid gases are not suspected, the Direct VFC Sampling System may be used.
Perform an MDL determination following the requirements in 40 CFR Part 136 Appendix B.
Analyze each canister in a manner identical to the field samples.
Prepare a canister spiked with VFCs used for calibration (native target compounds) at the VFC specific MDLs.
Analyze this canister to confirm that target compounds meet the qualitative retention time identification criteria in Table OTM-50-4.
Prepare and analyze a canister spiked at the midpoint of the ICAL curve seven times.
Calculate the average percent recovery using the same set of replicate data generated for Section 9.2.3.5.
Establish a target concentration for the QRL based on the intended use of the method.
Assess the results from the lowest calibration concentration canister sample and determine if it meets accuracy criteria of ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
Allow the nominal standard mixture to flow and equilibrate with humidified zero air for a minimum of 30 minutes in the mixing chamber.
Sample the gas standard mixture with a canister.
Ensure the final pressure of the calibration standard canister does not exceed the maximum pressure permitted by the GC/MS pre-concentrator unit.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 7 in Section 12 of this method.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to a clean evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as gases are added.
Add the VFC calibration standard and the zero air diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.

Tune/optimize the MS to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications upon initial installation of the instrument and following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities.
Verify the MS tune according to the manufacturer’s specifications prior to initial calibration.
Optional tune verification using bromofluorobenzene (BFB).
Add the Internal Standard (IS) through a dedicated non-sample port in the pre-concentrator at the same concentration for each injection.
Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion (base peak) unless there is a spectral interference.
Demonstrate that the response of the target analytes is not affected by the amount of CO2 in the canister samples during IDC and prior to each sample analysis.
Prepare two additional calibration standards at the lowest and highest points on the calibration curve and dilute the canisters with CO2.
Analyze the two standards containing CO2 using the same injection volume that will be used to analyze samples.
Ensure recovery of each target compound in the two standards containing CO2 is within ±30% of the true value for the compound.
Organize and load the canister samples for instrumental analysis.
Record sample names or identifiers, canister pressure, sample description, and total milliliters (mL) used for analysis in laboratory notebooks.
Connect samples to instrument sampler/autosampler and check for leaks.

If the system is leak free, open the canister valves prior to starting the analytical sequence. If a leak is detected, re-tighten the connection and perform another leak check. Do not open the canister valve if the leak check fails.

Generate an analytical sequence and sample batch that at a minimum includes one LB at the beginning of the sequence, a daily CCV after the LB, and field samples, including one duplicate analysis of a field sample.
Run the sample sequence.

At the conclusion of data acquisition, use the same software settings established during the calibration procedure to qualitatively identify analyte peaks in the predetermined retention time windows. Confirm the identity of each analyte by comparing its retention time with that of the corresponding target analyte peak in an ICAL standard or CCV.

Confirm that the ions are consistent with the continuing calibration results. The signals for all characteristic masses shall be present and reach a maximum within the same two consecutive scans. The retention time difference between the target compound in the sample and the CCV shall agree within ±6 scans or ±6 seconds (whichever is greater) of this difference in the CCV standard.
Inspect/tighten train components/connections and retry leak check.
Void any test run that doesn’t pass post leak-check.

preconditions.text: If there is a regulatory or programmatic lowest quantitative reporting requirement, the laboratory calibration curve must be set at or below this level.
Before and after each test run, ensure zero flow from the outlet of the bypass pump.

constraints.text: Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316) or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel.
Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316).
Analysis results from these seven replicates must have a percent relative standard deviation (%RSD) of the concentrations no greater than 25% for all method analytes.
The average percent recovery for each analyte must be within ±30% of the concentration prepared in Section 9.2.3.5.
Lowest calibration concentrations must be no lower than three times the MDL determined in 9.2.3.3.
The lowest calibration concentration may be established by a laboratory to meet project specific objectives.

The concentration of IS added to each injection should be in the lower middle portion of the calibration range (e.g., 5 ppbv) but must not exceed the concentration of the highest calibration level (e.g., 20 ppbv).
